title: scikit-learn integration
text: RieszBooster is an sklearn.base.BaseEstimator. It composes with cross_val_predict, GridSearchCV, Pipeline, clone, and the rest of sklearn.model_selection.

title: scikit-learn integration
text:
Cross-fitting
Plug-in \(\hat\alpha\) in a one-step / TMLE / DML estimator must be out-of-fold. Use cross_val_predict:

PythonR



import numpy as np, pandas as pd
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_predict
from rieszboost import RieszBooster, ATE

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
n = 2000
x = rng.uniform(0, 1, n)
pi = 1 / (1 + np.exp(-(-0.02 * x - x**2 + 4 * np.log(x + 0.3) + 1.5)))
a = rng.binomial(1, pi)
df = pd.DataFrame({"a": a.astype(float), "x": x})

booster = RieszBooster(
    estimand=ATE(),
    n_estimators=2000,
    early_stopping_rounds=20,
    validation_fraction=0.2,
    learning_rate=0.05,
    max_depth=4,
)

alpha_oof = cross_val_predict(booster, df, cv=5)
alpha_oof.shape, alpha_oof[:5]

((2000,), array([-3.6196816, -1.6876634, -1.0861515, -1.0516312,  1.2505229],
      dtype=float32))





set.seed(0)
n  <- 2000
x  <- runif(n)
pi <- 1 / (1 + exp(-(-0.02 * x - x^2 + 4 * log(x + 0.3) + 1.5)))
a  <- rbinom(n, 1, pi)
df <- data.frame(a = as.numeric(a), x = x)

# Call sklearn directly via reticulate, passing the underlying Python
# estimator from `booster$py`.
sk_ms <- reticulate::import("sklearn.model_selection", convert = FALSE)
booster <- RieszBooster$new(
  estimand = ATE(),
  n_estimators = 2000L,
  early_stopping_rounds = 20L,
  validation_fraction = 0.2,
  learning_rate = 0.05,
  max_depth = 4L
)
alpha_oof <- as.numeric(reticulate::py_to_r(
  sk_ms$cross_val_predict(booster$py, reticulate::r_to_py(df), cv = 5L)
))
length(alpha_oof); head(alpha_oof)

[1] 2000


[1]  1.301584  6.205725  2.930262 -3.024208  1.465699 -1.293439





Each fold runs its own internal early-stopping split.

title: scikit-learn integration
text:
Hyperparameter search
RieszBooster.score(X) returns negative held-out Riesz loss, so GridSearchCV selects the configuration with the lowest loss.

PythonR



from sklearn.model_selection import GridSearchCV

grid = GridSearchCV(
    RieszBooster(
        estimand=ATE(),
        n_estimators=300,
        early_stopping_rounds=20,
        validation_fraction=0.2,
    ),
    param_grid={"learning_rate": [0.03, 0.05, 0.1], "max_depth": [3, 4, 5]},
    cv=3,
    n_jobs=1,
).fit(df)

print("best params:", grid.best_params_)

best params: {'learning_rate': 0.05, 'max_depth': 3}

print(f"best score (neg Riesz loss): {grid.best_score_:.4f}")

best score (neg Riesz loss): 5.8153

best = grid.best_estimator_
alpha_hat = best.predict(df)
alpha_hat[:5]

array([-2.770411 , -1.6573501, -1.064923 , -1.0326685,  1.2886328],
      dtype=float32)





sk_ms <- reticulate::import("sklearn.model_selection", convert = FALSE)
base  <- RieszBooster$new(
  estimand = ATE(),
  n_estimators = 300L,
  early_stopping_rounds = 20L,
  validation_fraction = 0.2
)
grid <- sk_ms$GridSearchCV(
  base$py,
  param_grid = reticulate::dict(
    learning_rate = c(0.03, 0.05, 0.1),
    max_depth = list(3L, 4L, 5L)
  ),
  cv = 3L,
  n_jobs = 1L
)
grid$fit(reticulate::r_to_py(df))

GridSearchCV(cv=3,
             estimator=RieszBooster(early_stopping_rounds=20,
                                    estimand=Estimand(feature_keys=('a', 'x'),
                                                      m=<function ATE.<locals>.m at 0x1094718a0>,
                                                      extra_keys=(),
                                                      name='ATE',
                                                      factory_spec={'args': {'covariates': ['x'],
                                                                             'treatment': 'a'},
                                                                    'factory': 'ATE'}),
                                    n_estimators=300, validation_fraction=0.2),
             n_jobs=1,
             param_grid={'learning_rate': [0.03, 0.05, 0.1],
                         'max_depth': [3, 4, 5]})

cat("best params:\n"); print(reticulate::py_to_r(grid$best_params_))

best params:


$learning_rate
[1] 0.1

$max_depth
[1] 3

cat(sprintf("best score (neg Riesz loss): %.4f\n",
            reticulate::py_to_r(grid$best_score_)))

best score (neg Riesz loss): 6.8145

title: scikit-learn integration
text:
Other sklearn integrations
RieszBooster exposes the standard BaseEstimator interface:

clone(booster) returns a fresh unfitted copy with the same hyperparameters.
Pipeline wraps a feature-engineering step in front of the booster.
HalvingGridSearchCV, RandomizedSearchCV, BayesSearchCV (skopt) work without modification.
get_params() / set_params() are used by the search classes to introspect and mutate the configuration.

title: scikit-learn integration
text:
See also

examples/lee_schuler/binary_dgp.py builds an EEE estimator using cross_val_predict to produce out-of-fold \(\hat\alpha\).
Diagnostics and tuning covers which hyperparameters to vary in a grid search.

title: Quickstart — ATE in 30 lines
text:
Simulate
Binary treatment with known propensity \(\pi(x)\). We will recover \(\alpha_0(a, x) = a/\pi(x) - (1-a)/(1-\pi(x))\) without estimating \(\pi\).

import pandas as pd
from rieszboost import RieszBooster, ATE

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
n = 4000
x = rng.uniform(0, 1, n)
pi = 1 / (1 + np.exp(-(-0.02 * x - x**2 + 4 * np.log(x + 0.3) + 1.5)))
a = rng.binomial(1, pi)
df = pd.DataFrame({"a": a.astype(float), "x": x})

title: Quickstart — ATE in 30 lines
section: Predict and compare to the closed form
text:
Predict and compare to the closed form
We know \(\alpha_0\) here, so we can score the fit directly.

PythonR



alpha_hat  = booster.predict(df)
true_alpha = a / pi - (1 - a) / (1 - pi)
print(f"corr(α̂, α₀) = {np.corrcoef(alpha_hat, true_alpha)[0, 1]:.3f}")

corr(α̂, α₀) = 0.934

print(f"RMSE         = {np.sqrt(np.mean((alpha_hat - true_alpha)**2)):.3f}")

RMSE         = 0.973





alpha_hat  <- booster$predict(df)
true_alpha <- a / pi - (1 - a) / (1 - pi)
cat(sprintf("corr(alpha_hat, alpha_0) = %.3f\n",
            cor(alpha_hat, true_alpha)))

corr(alpha_hat, alpha_0) = 0.962

cat(sprintf("RMSE                     = %.3f\n",
            sqrt(mean((alpha_hat - true_alpha)^2))))

RMSE                     = 0.768

title: Quickstart — ATE in 30 lines
text:
Visual sanity check

PythonR



import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.scatter(true_alpha, alpha_hat, s=4, alpha=0.4)
lim = max(abs(true_alpha).max(), abs(alpha_hat).max())
ax.plot([-lim, lim], [-lim, lim], "k--", lw=1)
ax.set_xlabel(r"$\alpha_0$ (closed-form)")
ax.set_ylabel(r"$\hat\alpha$ (rieszboost)")
plt.show()




Estimated Riesz representer vs. the closed-form ground truth on the synthetic ATE DGP.

plot(true_alpha, alpha_hat, pch = 16, cex = 0.4, col = adjustcolor("black", 0.4),
     xlim = c(-lim, lim), ylim = c(-lim, lim),

title: Install
text:
R wrapper (optional)
The R package is an R6 wrapper that calls the Python implementation through reticulate. It does not require any separate algorithmic dependency.
First-time setup:

Install the R package dependencies:
install.packages(c("reticulate", "R6"))
Load the R wrapper. For this guide we dev-load it from the source tree:
pkgload::load_all("r/rieszboost")
Point reticulate at the Python venv:
use_python_rieszboost(".venv/bin/python")
Or set the environment variable before R starts:
export RETICULATE_PYTHON=/path/to/rieszboost/.venv/bin/python

The R API exposes every built-in estimand, both losses, both backends, and the full RieszBooster interface. The symbolic tracer for custom m() is Python-only; see r-interface for how to call it from R.

title: Install
text:
Tests
.venv/bin/python -m pytest python/tests -v

Rscript -e '
  Sys.setenv(RETICULATE_PYTHON = file.path(getwd(), ".venv/bin/python"))
  pkgload::load_all("r/rieszboost")
  testthat::test_dir("r/rieszboost/tests/testthat")
'

title: Estimands
text:
An Estimand describes the linear functional you want to fit. It holds three pieces of information: the column names \(\alpha\) is indexed by (feature_keys), any per-row payload columns \(m\) needs such as pre-sampled treatments for stochastic interventions (extra_keys), and the m(z, alpha) callable.
This page covers the six built-in factories and how to write your own.

title: Estimands
text:
Built-in factories



Factory
\(m(z, \alpha)\)
Notes




ATE(treatment, covariates)
\(\alpha(1, x) - \alpha(0, x)\)
Average treatment effect — see examples/lee_schuler/binary_dgp.py


ATT(treatment, covariates)
\(a \cdot (\alpha(1, x) - \alpha(0, x))\)
ATT partial parameter. Full ATT divides by \(P(A=1)\) — combine with a delta-method EIF downstream. See examples/lee_schuler/binary_dgp.py.


TSM(level, treatment, covariates)
\(\alpha(\text{level}, x)\)
Treatment-specific mean — see examples/tsm.py


AdditiveShift(delta, ...)
\(\alpha(a + \delta, x) - \alpha(a, x)\)
Continuous-treatment shift effect — see examples/lee_schuler/continuous_dgp.py


LocalShift(delta, threshold, ...)
\(\mathbf{1}\{a < \text{threshold}\} \cdot (\alpha(a + \delta, x) - \alpha(a, x))\)
LASE partial parameter; same caveat as ATT. See examples/lee_schuler/continuous_dgp.py.


StochasticIntervention(samples_key, ...)
\(\frac{1}{K} \sum_k \alpha(a'_k, x)\)
IPSI via Monte Carlo over the intervention density — see examples/stochastic_intervention.py



The R and Python factories take the same arguments.

PythonR



from rieszboost import (
    ATE, ATT, TSM, AdditiveShift, LocalShift, StochasticIntervention,
)
ATE(treatment="a", covariates=("x",)).name

'ATE'

TSM(level=1, treatment="a", covariates=("x",)).name

'TSM(level=1)'

LocalShift(delta=0.3, threshold=0.7).name

'LocalShift(delta=0.3, threshold=0.7)'





ATE(treatment = "a", covariates = "x")

Estimand(feature_keys=('a', 'x'), m=<function ATE.<locals>.m at 0x117da8e00>, extra_keys=(), name='ATE', factory_spec={'factory': 'ATE', 'args': {'treatment': 'a', 'covariates': ['x']}})
 signature: (z, alpha)

TSM(level = 1, treatment = "a", covariates = "x")

Estimand(feature_keys=('a', 'x'), m=<function TSM.<locals>.m at 0x117e5fba0>, extra_keys=(), name='TSM(level=1.0)', factory_spec={'factory': 'TSM', 'args': {'level': 1.0, 'treatment': 'a', 'covariates': ['x']}})
 signature: (z, alpha)

LocalShift(delta = 0.3, threshold = 0.7)

Estimand(feature_keys=('a', 'x'), m=<function LocalShift.<locals>.m at 0x117e7b600>, extra_keys=(), name='LocalShift(delta=0.3, threshold=0.7)', factory_spec={'factory': 'LocalShift', 'args': {'delta': 0.3, 'threshold': 0.7, 'treatment': 'a', 'covariates': ['x']}})
 signature: (z, alpha)

title: Estimands
text:
Stochastic intervention: pre-sample, then fit
StochasticIntervention needs a list of treatment values drawn from the intervention density on each row. Build that column once before fitting.

PythonR



import numpy as np, pandas as pd
from rieszboost import RieszBooster, StochasticIntervention

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
n = 1000
x = rng.uniform(0, 1, n)
a = rng.uniform(0, 1, n)
df = pd.DataFrame({"a": a, "x": x})

# Modified-treatment policy: a' ~ Normal(a + 0.1, 0.2), 20 samples per row
df["shift_samples"] = [
    rng.normal(a_i + 0.1, 0.2, size=20) for a_i in df["a"]
]

booster = RieszBooster(
    estimand=StochasticIntervention(samples_key="shift_samples"),
    n_estimators=200, learning_rate=0.05, max_depth=4,
).fit(df)

booster.predict(df).mean()

np.float32(0.35250518)





set.seed(0)
n  <- 1000
x  <- runif(n); a <- runif(n)
df <- data.frame(a = a, x = x)

# list-column with 20 samples per row from Normal(a + 0.1, 0.2)
df$shift_samples <- lapply(df$a, function(a_i) rnorm(20, a_i + 0.1, 0.2))

booster <- RieszBooster$new(
  estimand = StochasticIntervention(samples_key = "shift_samples"),
  n_estimators = 200L, learning_rate = 0.05, max_depth = 4L
)
booster$fit(df)
mean(booster$predict(df))

[1] 0.3499112





StochasticIntervention sets extra_keys=("shift_samples",) so RieszBooster.fit(df) pulls that column through to m along with the features.

title: Estimands
text:
Custom estimands
To fit a functional not covered by the built-ins, write m(z, alpha) and wrap it in an Estimand. The library traces \(m\) to extract the linear-form structure.
\(m\) must be a finite linear combination of point evaluations of \(\alpha\), built with +, -, scalar *, and calls to alpha(...). Squaring, adding constants, integrals, and other non-linear operations raise.






NotePython-only



Custom m() is Python-only. From R, define m in Python via reticulate::py_run_string and pass the resulting Estimand to RieszBooster$new(estimand=...). See the R interface page for the recipe.



from rieszboost import Estimand, RieszBooster
import numpy as np, pandas as pd

# A weighted contrast: 2x the ATE at level a=2 minus the level at a=1.
def m_weighted_contrast(z, alpha):
    return 2.0 * (alpha(a=2, x=z["x"]) - alpha(a=0, x=z["x"])) \
           - alpha(a=1, x=z["x"])

est = Estimand(
    feature_keys=("a", "x"),
    m=m_weighted_contrast,
    name="WeightedContrast",
)
print(est)

Estimand(feature_keys=('a', 'x'), m=<function m_weighted_contrast at 0x13470d8a0>, extra_keys=(), name='WeightedContrast', factory_spec=None)


Pass the estimand to RieszBooster:

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
df = pd.DataFrame({
    "a": rng.choice([0, 1, 2], size=500).astype(float),
    "x": rng.uniform(0, 1, 500),
})

booster = RieszBooster(estimand=est, n_estimators=100, max_depth=3).fit(df)
booster.predict(df)[:5]

array([ 4.630866 , -2.446456 , -2.5141447, -4.992177 , -4.992177 ],
      dtype=float32)



What the tracer rejects
The errors point at the offending operation:

from rieszboost import Estimand, RieszBooster, trace

def bad_squared(z, alpha):
    return alpha(a=1, x=z["x"]) ** 2  # nonlinear in alpha

trace(Estimand(feature_keys=("a", "x"), m=bad_squared), {"a": 0, "x": 0.5})

TypeError: unsupported operand type(s) for ** or pow(): 'LinearForm' and 'int'



def bad_constant(z, alpha):
    return alpha(a=1, x=z["x"]) + 1.0  # constant offset

trace(Estimand(feature_keys=("a", "x"), m=bad_constant), {"a": 0, "x": 0.5})

TypeError: Cannot add a scalar to a LinearForm — m must be linear in alpha (no constant offsets).


For estimands with integrals or derivatives of \(\alpha\) (outside the finite-linear-form algebra), Monte-Carlo-sample and use StochasticIntervention if applicable.

title: Estimands
text:
Feature keys, extra keys
The Estimand dataclass has these fields:
@dataclass
class Estimand:
    feature_keys: tuple[str, ...]      # columns alpha is indexed by
    m: Callable[..., Any]              # the linear functional
    extra_keys: tuple[str, ...] = ()   # per-row payload columns (e.g. shift_samples)
    name: str = "custom"
    factory_spec: dict | None = None   # set by built-in factories so the estimand
                                       # round-trips through save / load
RieszBooster reads feature_keys and extra_keys to assemble the row dicts passed to m.

title: Developing rieszboost
text:
rieszboost/
├── python/
│   ├── rieszboost/              # the library

title: Developing rieszboost
text:
Tracer
The user writes m(z, alpha) with +, -, scalar *, and calls to alpha(...). The library passes a Tracer in as alpha; each call returns a single-term LinearForm, and Python’s operator overloading composes them. The result has .as_pairs() returning [(coef, point), ...], which build_augmented consumes to construct the augmented dataset. Operations outside the linear-form algebra (squaring, integrals, constants) raise at trace time. See python/rieszboost/tracer.py.

title: Developing rieszboost
Developing rieszboost
text:
Adding a LossSpec
Implement the LossSpec protocol from losses.py. SquaredLoss is the shortest template; KLLoss shows how to handle a non-identity link.

Add the class to python/rieszboost/losses.py.
Export from __init__.py.
Add an R wrapper.
Add a runnable example to docs/backends-and-losses.qmd.
Test in python/tests/test_bregman.py.

title: Developing rieszboost
text:
Adding a backend
Implement the Backend protocol from python/rieszboost/backends/base.py. One method, fit_augmented(...), returning a FitResult whose predictor implements predict_eta and predict_alpha. XGBoostBackend and SklearnBackend are reference implementations.
The backend sees an AugmentedDataset and a LossSpec. It does not need to know about the estimand or the tracer.

title: Developing rieszboost
text:
Living-doc rule
Public-API changes update both README.md and the relevant docs/*.qmd page in the same commit. On bilingual pages, update both the Python and R tabs.
The pre-commit hook at .githooks/pre-commit enforces this. Activate per clone:
git config core.hooksPath .githooks
If any of these files are staged:

python/rieszboost/{__init__,estimator,estimand,augmentation,losses,diagnostics,tracer}.py
python/rieszboost/backends/*.py
r/rieszboost/R/*.R

then at least one of these must also be staged:

docs/*.qmd
docs/_quarto.yml
README.md

The hook also greps the staged diff for two doc anti-patterns and blocks if it finds them: design-decision metacommentary (sentences describing the API’s negative space, e.g. what’s omitted, deferred, or rejected) and AI-flavored hedges (editorial framing, sweeping qualifiers, ornamental noun phrases). The hook’s error message lists concrete bad/good rewrite pairs. Match the ngboost user guide tone: direct, instructive, short.
For internal refactors that don’t touch any signature, test additions, or comment fixes: git commit --no-verify.

title: Backends and losses
text: RieszBooster takes three orthogonal configuration objects: an estimand (the linear functional you want to fit), a loss (the Bregman-Riesz objective), and a backend (the gradient-boosting implementation). The defaults are XGBoostBackend and SquaredLoss. This page covers when to swap them.

title: Backends and losses
text:
The default backend: XGBoostBackend
Each row \(z_i\) contributes (a) itself with quadratic coefficient \(a=1\), \(b=0\), and (b) one augmented row at every point \(\alpha\) is evaluated at by \(m(z_i, \cdot)\), with \(a=0\) and \(b = -2 \cdot \text{coef}\). The Riesz loss becomes a per-row weighted regression that xgboost handles via its custom-objective interface.
XGBoostBackend has two knobs of its own:
XGBoostBackend(hessian_floor=2.0, gradient_only=False)
hessian_floor is the lower bound on the per-row Hessian fed to xgboost. Counterfactual rows have \(H = 2a = 0\); without a floor, xgboost’s leaf-weight Newton step (-G/(H+λ)) is degenerate at those leaves. The default of 2.0 matches the natural Hessian on original rows.
gradient_only short-circuits the second-order Hessian and uses \(H = 1\) everywhere — first-order gradient boosting (Friedman 2001 / Lee-Schuler Algorithm 2). Set True to reproduce the Lee-Schuler reference implementation exactly.
max_depth, reg_lambda, subsample, learning_rate, n_estimators, and early_stopping_rounds are passed through from the RieszBooster constructor.

title: Backends and losses
text:
SklearnBackend
To use a non-tree base learner, pass a base_learner_factory: a zero-arg callable returning a fresh sklearn-compatible regressor each round.
The backend implements first-order gradient boosting with closed-form line search. Each round: fit the weak learner to the negative gradient of the Riesz loss, solve for the optimal step size under a quadratic surrogate, update the running prediction.

PythonR



import numpy as np, pandas as pd
from sklearn.kernel_ridge import KernelRidge
from rieszboost import RieszBooster, ATE, SklearnBackend

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
n = 1000
x = rng.uniform(0, 1, n)
pi = 1 / (1 + np.exp(-(8 * x - 4)))
a = rng.binomial(1, pi)
df = pd.DataFrame({"a": a.astype(float), "x": x})

booster = RieszBooster(
    estimand=ATE(),
    backend=SklearnBackend(lambda: KernelRidge(alpha=1.0, kernel="rbf", gamma=2.0)),
    n_estimators=80,
    learning_rate=0.05,
    early_stopping_rounds=10,
    validation_fraction=0.2,
).fit(df)

alpha_hat  = booster.predict(df)
true_alpha = a / pi - (1 - a) / (1 - pi)
print(f"corr = {np.corrcoef(alpha_hat, true_alpha)[0,1]:.3f}, "
      f"RMSE = {np.sqrt(np.mean((alpha_hat - true_alpha)**2)):.3f}")

corr = 0.913, RMSE = 1.663





set.seed(0)
n  <- 1000
x  <- runif(n)
pi <- 1 / (1 + exp(-(8 * x - 4)))
a  <- rbinom(n, 1, pi)
df <- data.frame(a = as.numeric(a), x = x)

# Build the Python factory directly via reticulate so each round gets a
# fresh sklearn object.
sk_kr <- reticulate::import("sklearn.kernel_ridge", convert = FALSE)
factory <- reticulate::py_func(function() sk_kr$KernelRidge(alpha = 1.0,
                                                             kernel = "rbf",
                                                             gamma = 2.0))

booster <- RieszBooster$new(
  estimand = ATE(),
  backend = SklearnBackend(base_learner_factory = factory),
  n_estimators = 80L,
  learning_rate = 0.05,
  early_stopping_rounds = 10L,
  validation_fraction = 0.2
)
booster$fit(df)

alpha_hat  <- booster$predict(df)
true_alpha <- a / pi - (1 - a) / (1 - pi)
cat(sprintf("corr = %.3f, RMSE = %.3f\n",
            cor(alpha_hat, true_alpha),
            sqrt(mean((alpha_hat - true_alpha)^2))))

corr = 0.928, RMSE = 1.579





SklearnBackend is slower than XGBoostBackend (no parallel tree splits). Use it when the data is better fit by a non-tree learner (e.g. low-dimensional problems where kernel ridge dominates), or when xgboost is unavailable in your environment.






TipSkipping xgboost



SklearnBackend does not import xgboost. The library lazy-imports xgboost only when XGBoostBackend is used, so you can run rieszboost in xgboost-hostile environments (alpine containers, builds without OpenMP) by passing backend=SklearnBackend(...) and never installing xgboost.

title: Backends and losses
text:
Losses










Loss
Potential \(\phi\)
Link \(\eta \to \alpha\)
Domain
Use for




SquaredLoss
\(t^2\)
identity
\(\mathbb{R}\)
Default.


KLLoss
\(t \log t\)
exp
\((0, \infty)\)
Density-ratio targets (TSM, IPSI). Guarantees \(\hat\alpha > 0\). Requires non-negative \(m\)-coefficients.


BernoulliLoss
\(t \log t + (1-t)\log(1-t)\)
sigmoid
\((0, 1)\)
\(\alpha_0\) known to lie in \((0, 1)\). Same coefficient-sign restriction as KLLoss.


BoundedSquaredLoss(lo, hi)
\(t^2\) + sigmoid-scaled link
\(\mathrm{lo} + (\mathrm{hi}-\mathrm{lo}) \cdot \sigma\)
\((\mathrm{lo}, \mathrm{hi})\)
Squared-loss fit with prior bounds (e.g. trimmed-propensity ratios).



SquaredLoss is the standard Lee-Schuler / Chernozhukov objective. Per-row loss in \(\alpha\)-space: \(a \cdot \alpha^2 + b \cdot \alpha\).
KLLoss (\(\phi = t \log t\), exp link) targets density ratios. The exponential link keeps \(\hat\alpha > 0\).

PythonR



from rieszboost import KLLoss, TSM, RieszBooster

booster = RieszBooster(
    estimand=TSM(level=1),
    loss=KLLoss(max_eta=50),
    n_estimators=300,
    learning_rate=0.05,
    max_depth=3,
).fit(df)

alpha_hat = booster.predict(df)
print(f"min α̂ = {alpha_hat.min():.4f}")

min α̂ = 0.1174

print(f"mean α̂ on treated rows = {alpha_hat[a == 1].mean():.3f}")

mean α̂ on treated rows = 1.845





booster <- RieszBooster$new(
  estimand = TSM(level = 1),
  loss = KLLoss(max_eta = 50),
  n_estimators = 300L,
  learning_rate = 0.05,
  max_depth = 3L
)
booster$fit(df)
alpha_hat <- booster$predict(df)
cat(sprintf("min alpha_hat = %.4f\n", min(alpha_hat)))

min alpha_hat = 0.1173

cat(sprintf("mean alpha_hat on treated rows = %.3f\n",
            mean(alpha_hat[a == 1])))

mean alpha_hat on treated rows = 1.867





KLLoss requires all augmented b coefficients \(\le 0\), equivalent to all \(m\)-coefficients being non-negative. The check happens at fit time and raises with a clear error if violated.






WarningWhen KLLoss can be unstable



Pure-counterfactual rows (\(a=0\), \(b<0\)) have no minimum in \(\eta\) under KL — leaf weights at low-overlap points can extrapolate. Use SquaredLoss unless the positivity guarantee is needed.



BoundedSquaredLoss for hard prior bounds
If you know \(\alpha_0 \in [\text{lo}, \text{hi}]\) (e.g. you’ve trimmed propensities to \([\varepsilon, 1-\varepsilon]\) so \(1/\pi \in [1, 1/\varepsilon]\)), BoundedSquaredLoss(lo, hi) keeps predictions in that box via a sigmoid-scaled link. The per-row loss in \(\alpha\)-space matches SquaredLoss; the link reparameterizes \(\eta\) so \(\hat\alpha\) saturates at the bounds rather than extrapolating past them.

PythonR



from rieszboost import BoundedSquaredLoss
booster = RieszBooster(
    estimand=TSM(level=1),
    loss=BoundedSquaredLoss(lo=0.0, hi=12.0),
    n_estimators=200, learning_rate=0.05, max_depth=3,
).fit(df)
alpha_hat = booster.predict(df)
print(f"alpha_hat in ({alpha_hat.min():.3f}, {alpha_hat.max():.3f})")

alpha_hat in (0.267, 11.751)





booster <- RieszBooster$new(
  estimand = TSM(level = 1),
  loss = BoundedSquaredLoss(lo = 0.0, hi = 12.0),
  n_estimators = 200L, learning_rate = 0.05, max_depth = 3L
)
booster$fit(df)
alpha_hat <- booster$predict(df)
cat(sprintf("alpha_hat in (%.3f, %.3f)\n",
            min(alpha_hat), max(alpha_hat)))

alpha_hat in (0.267, 11.812)











TipPick bounds tightly



Generous bounds make the sigmoid link saturate over most of \(\eta\), so boosting steps barely move predictions. Set lo and hi to your prior on \(\alpha_0\).


BernoulliLoss is the same construction with \(\text{lo}=0\), \(\text{hi}=1\) and the Bernoulli Bregman potential. Use it when \(\alpha_0\) is a probability.

title: Backends and losses
Diagnostics and tuning
text:
You don’t observe \(\alpha_0\) on real data, so the validation curve from early_stopping_rounds is the only training-time signal. After fitting, .diagnose() reports magnitude statistics and warnings about extreme tail values, near-positivity violations, and single-point extrapolation. Those warnings often matter more for downstream variance than the bulk fit quality.
This page walks through .diagnose() on an under-tuned booster (so the warnings fire) and maps each warning to the hyperparameter that fixes it.

title: Diagnostics and tuning
text:
Setup: a low-overlap dataset
We push the propensity close to 0 in part of the covariate space. This is where naive inverse-propensity weighting falls apart.

PythonR



import numpy as np, pandas as pd
from rieszboost import RieszBooster, ATE

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
n = 2000
x = rng.uniform(0, 1, n)
pi = 1 / (1 + np.exp(-(8.0 * x - 5.0)))
a = rng.binomial(1, pi)
df = pd.DataFrame({"a": a.astype(float), "x": x})
print(f"min π(x) = {pi.min():.3f}, fraction with π(x) < 0.05 = {(pi < 0.05).mean():.2f}")

min π(x) = 0.007, fraction with π(x) < 0.05 = 0.26





set.seed(0)
n  <- 2000
x  <- runif(n)
pi <- 1 / (1 + exp(-(8 * x - 5)))
a  <- rbinom(n, 1, pi)
df <- data.frame(a = as.numeric(a), x = x)
cat(sprintf("min pi(x) = %.3f, fraction with pi(x) < 0.05 = %.2f\n",
            min(pi), mean(pi < 0.05)))

min pi(x) = 0.007, fraction with pi(x) < 0.05 = 0.26

title: Diagnostics and tuning
text:
A greedy fit
Deep trees, no regularization, no early stopping. This will extrapolate badly in the low-π region.

PythonR



greedy = RieszBooster(
    estimand=ATE(),
    n_estimators=300,
    learning_rate=0.3,
    max_depth=8,
    reg_lambda=0.0,
).fit(df)

print(greedy.diagnose(df).summary())

Riesz representer diagnostics (n=2000):
  RMS magnitude   : 2.4655
  mean            : -0.1415
  min / max       : -11.5966 / 19.8519
  |alpha| quantiles:
     0.50: 1.1422
     0.90: 2.6658
     0.99: 10.2367
     1.00: 19.8519
  extreme rows    : 0/2000 (0.00%) with |alpha| > 30.0
  held-out Riesz  : -66.7209





greedy <- RieszBooster$new(
  estimand = ATE(),
  n_estimators = 300L,
  learning_rate = 0.3,
  max_depth = 8L,
  reg_lambda = 0.0
)
greedy$fit(df)
cat(greedy$diagnose(df)$summary)

Riesz representer diagnostics (n=2000):
  RMS magnitude   : 2.3614
  mean            : -0.2094
  min / max       : -13.6254 / 17.4702
  |alpha| quantiles:
     0.50: 1.1424
     0.90: 2.6994
     0.99: 9.7480
     1.00: 17.4702
  extreme rows    : 0/2000 (0.00%) with |alpha| > 30.0
  held-out Riesz  : -62.8998

title: Diagnostics and tuning
text:
Reading the report
The summary has three parts.
Magnitude block. RMS, mean, min/max, and |alpha| quantiles. The 99th percentile and the max are the load-bearing numbers. Healthy fits have max no more than a few times the 99th percentile. Max far above the 99th percentile signals a single-point extrapolation outlier: one leaf is far from any training data and its prediction has run away.
Extreme rows. Rows where \(|\hat\alpha|\) exceeds extreme_threshold (default 30). Downstream IF estimator variance is dominated by the largest few weights, so even 1% of rows over the threshold inflates confidence intervals.
Warnings. Two automatic checks:

“X% of rows have \(|\hat\alpha| >\) threshold — possible near-positivity violation.” Either the data has genuine near-positivity (truncate extreme_threshold and report the loss of overlap), or the booster is over-fitting (tune as below).
“max \(|\hat\alpha|\) is >10× the 99th percentile — likely a single extrapolation outlier.” The booster found a leaf with no nearby training data. Tighten trees, increase reg_lambda, or stop earlier.

title: Diagnostics and tuning
text:
A tuned fit
Same data with sensible hyperparameters and early stopping.

PythonR



tuned = RieszBooster(
    estimand=ATE(),
    n_estimators=2000,
    learning_rate=0.05,
    max_depth=3,
    reg_lambda=1.0,
    early_stopping_rounds=20,
    validation_fraction=0.2,
).fit(df)

print(f"stopped at iter {tuned.best_iteration_}, "
      f"best held-out Riesz loss = {tuned.best_score_:.4f}")

stopped at iter 1999, best held-out Riesz loss = -31.0978

print()
print(tuned.diagnose(df).summary())

Riesz representer diagnostics (n=2000):
  RMS magnitude   : 3.2958
  mean            : 0.0136
  min / max       : -43.5758 / 30.7376
  |alpha| quantiles:
     0.50: 1.1028
     0.90: 2.9091
     0.99: 14.2924
     1.00: 43.5758
  extreme rows    : 2/2000 (0.10%) with |alpha| > 30.0
  held-out Riesz  : -28.1668





tuned <- RieszBooster$new(
  estimand = ATE(),
  n_estimators = 2000L,
  learning_rate = 0.05,
  max_depth = 3L,
  reg_lambda = 1.0,
  early_stopping_rounds = 20L,
  validation_fraction = 0.2
)
tuned$fit(df)
cat(sprintf("stopped at iter %d, best held-out Riesz loss = %.4f\n\n",
            reticulate::py_to_r(tuned$py$best_iteration_),
            reticulate::py_to_r(tuned$py$best_score_)))

stopped at iter 1999, best held-out Riesz loss = -29.3481

cat(tuned$diagnose(df)$summary)

Riesz representer diagnostics (n=2000):
  RMS magnitude   : 3.1136
  mean            : -0.0701
  min / max       : -15.5357 / 43.7492
  |alpha| quantiles:
     0.50: 1.1113
     0.90: 3.2547
     0.99: 10.1967
     1.00: 43.7492
  extreme rows    : 3/2000 (0.15%) with |alpha| > 30.0
  held-out Riesz  : -30.6263





The max \(|\hat\alpha|\) shrinks by an order of magnitude, the extreme-row count drops, and the warnings clear.

title: Diagnostics and tuning
text:
Tuning recipe









Knob
Default
When to change
Effect




max_depth
4
Drop to 2–3 for extrapolation outliers
Shallow trees can’t carve out a high-magnitude leaf for a single point.


learning_rate
0.05
Drop to 0.02 for harder problems
Smaller updates per tree; pair with higher n_estimators.


n_estimators + early_stopping_rounds
200, None
Set n_estimators=1000-2000 and early_stopping_rounds=20
The validation split picks the iteration count.


validation_fraction
0.0
Set to 0.2 with early_stopping_rounds
Internal split for early stopping. Alternative: pass eval_set= explicitly.


reg_lambda
1.0
Bump to 5–10 for low-overlap data
xgboost L2 on leaf weights; damps the magnitude of any single leaf.


subsample
1.0
Try 0.5–0.8 with very large \(n\)
Stochastic boosting.



XGBoostBackend exposes two more knobs:








Knob
Default
When to change




gradient_only
False
Set True to reproduce Lee-Schuler Algorithm 2 / Friedman 2001 exactly.


hessian_floor
2.0
Lower bound on the per-row Hessian. The default matches the natural Hessian of original-data rows.



See backends and losses for the backend protocol.

title: Diagnostics and tuning
text:
What .diagnose() does not do
It does not estimate \(\alpha_0\) for comparison; that closed form is generally unavailable. It does not assess sample size. It checks magnitude and the two failure modes above. For fit quality, track the held-out Riesz loss (best_score_). For downstream confidence intervals, use the bootstrap on the plug-in estimator.

title: rieszboost
text:
rieszboost estimates the Riesz representer \(\alpha_0\) of a linear functional \(\theta(P) = \mathbb{E}[m(Z, g_0)]\), the building block of one-step, TMLE, and DML estimators in semiparametric inference. It implements Lee & Schuler, RieszBoost (arXiv:2501.04871) with an sklearn-style API in Python and an R6 mirror in R.
R and Python predictions are bitwise-identical on the same input. This guide presents every code example in both languages; pick a tab and your choice persists across the site.

title: rieszboost
text:
Pages

What is a Riesz representer — concepts and motivation.
Install — Python install, optional R wrapper.
Quickstart — end-to-end ATE on a synthetic dataset.
Estimands — built-in factories (ATE, ATT, TSM, AdditiveShift, LocalShift, StochasticIntervention) and custom m(z, alpha).
scikit-learn integration — cross_val_predict, GridSearchCV, Pipeline, clone.
Diagnostics and tuning — reading .diagnose() and choosing hyperparameters.
Backends and losses — XGBoostBackend, SklearnBackend; SquaredLoss, KLLoss, BernoulliLoss, BoundedSquaredLoss.
Save and load — serialize fitted boosters across Python and R.
R interface — reticulate setup, R-side API, what’s currently Python-only.
Developing rieszboost — repo layout, tests, how to contribute.
References.







TipCode execution



Every code block in this guide ran at build time. The numbers and figures came from the version of rieszboost that produced this page.

title: What’s a Riesz representer, and why boost it?
text:
In semiparametric inference, you often want to estimate a functional of an unknown nuisance:
\[
\theta(P) \;=\; \mathbb{E}\!\left[m(Z, g_0)\right]
\]
where \(g_0(z) = \mathbb{E}[Y \mid Z = z]\) is an outcome regression and \(m(z, g)\) is a linear functional of \(g\). Examples:







Target
\(m(z, g)\)




Average treatment effect
\(g(1, x) - g(0, x)\)


Treatment-specific mean at \(a^\star\)
\(g(a^\star, x)\)


Additive shift effect \(a \mapsto a + \delta\)
\(g(a + \delta, x) - g(a, x)\)


Stochastic intervention \(a \sim h(\cdot \mid x)\)
\(\int g(a', x)\,h(a' \mid x)\,da'\)



The plug-in estimator \(\hat\theta = n^{-1} \sum_i m(Z_i, \hat g)\) is biased because \(\hat g\) is itself estimated. Modern semiparametric estimators (one-step, TMLE, DML) correct that bias by adding a term involving the Riesz representer \(\alpha_0\) of \(m\):
\[
\hat\theta_{\text{corrected}} \;=\; n^{-1} \sum_i \Big[\,m(Z_i, \hat g) + \alpha_0(Z_i)\,\big(Y_i - \hat g(Z_i)\big)\,\Big].
\]
crumbs: What's a Riesz representer, and why boost it?

title: What’s a Riesz representer, and why boost it?
text:
In semiparametric inference, you often want to estimate a functional of an unknown nuisance:
\[
\theta(P) \;=\; \mathbb{E}\!\left[m(Z, g_0)\right]
\]
where \(g_0(z) = \mathbb{E}[Y \mid Z = z]\) is an outcome regression and \(m(z, g)\) is a linear functional of \(g\). Examples:







Target
\(m(z, g)\)




Average treatment effect
\(g(1, x) - g(0, x)\)


Treatment-specific mean at \(a^\star\)
\(g(a^\star, x)\)


Additive shift effect \(a \mapsto a + \delta\)
\(g(a + \delta, x) - g(a, x)\)


Stochastic intervention \(a \sim h(\cdot \mid x)\)
\(\int g(a', x)\,h(a' \mid x)\,da'\)



The plug-in estimator \(\hat\theta = n^{-1} \sum_i m(Z_i, \hat g)\) is biased because \(\hat g\) is itself estimated. Modern semiparametric estimators (one-step, TMLE, DML) correct that bias by adding a term involving the Riesz representer \(\alpha_0\) of \(m\):
\[
\hat\theta_{\text{corrected}} \;=\; n^{-1} \sum_i \Big[\,m(Z_i, \hat g) + \alpha_0(Z_i)\,\big(Y_i - \hat g(Z_i)\big)\,\Big].
\]
crumbs: What's a Riesz representer, and why boost it?

title: What’s a Riesz representer, and why boost it?
text:
What is \(\alpha_0\)?
By the Riesz representation theorem, since \(m(z, \cdot)\) is bounded and linear in \(g\), there exists a function \(\alpha_0(z)\) satisfying
\[
\mathbb{E}[m(Z, g)] \;=\; \mathbb{E}\!\left[\alpha_0(Z) \cdot g(Z)\right]
\quad \text{for all } g.
\]
For ATE, \(\alpha_0(a, x) = a/\pi(x) - (1-a)/(1-\pi(x))\) is the inverse-propensity weight. For TSM, \(\alpha_0\) is the density ratio \(\mathbb{1}\{a = a^\star\}/\pi(a^\star \mid x)\). In general, \(\alpha_0\) has a closed form involving the generalized propensity score.
crumbs: What's a Riesz representer, and why boost it?

title: What’s a Riesz representer, and why boost it?
section: Why not estimate the propensity directly?
text:
Why not estimate the propensity directly?
For ATE you can: estimate \(\hat\pi(x)\), plug in, get \(\hat\alpha(a, x) = a/\hat\pi(x) - (1-a)/(1-\hat\pi(x))\).
This breaks down when:

Positivity is borderline. If \(\hat\pi(x)\) approaches 0 or 1, \(\hat\alpha\) explodes. Truncation patches the symptom but introduces its own bias.
The estimand is non-standard. For continuous shifts, stochastic interventions, and longitudinal LMTPs, the closed form involves derivatives of the conditional density of \(A\) given \(X\). Those are harder to estimate than \(\alpha_0\) itself.
Misspecification compounds. Plug-in error in \(\hat\pi\) propagates nonlinearly through \(1/\hat\pi\).
crumbs: What's a Riesz representer, and why boost it?

title: What’s a Riesz representer, and why boost it?
text:
Riesz regression
Chernozhukov et al. (2021) observed that \(\alpha_0\) is the unique minimizer of the Riesz loss
\[
L(\alpha) \;=\; \mathbb{E}\!\left[\alpha(Z)^2 - 2\,m(Z, \alpha)\right]
\]
over all square-integrable \(\alpha\). The Gateaux derivative at \(\alpha_0\) vanishes by the Riesz identity. Estimating \(\alpha_0\) becomes a regression problem with a custom loss: no propensity, no density derivative, no truncation.
The minimizer is well-defined regardless of the closed-form tails. Where \(\pi(x)\) is small, the squared-loss penalty shrinks \(\hat\alpha\) toward zero in low-overlap regions, which controls downstream variance.
crumbs: What's a Riesz representer, and why boost it?

title: What’s a Riesz representer, and why boost it?
text:
Why boost it?
Lee & Schuler (2025) show gradient boosting fits the Riesz loss well:

Tabular data. Boosting is the standard tool for tabular regression. Hyperparameters that work for outcome models transfer to Riesz regression.
Direct gradient and Hessian. When \(m\) is a finite linear combination of point evaluations of \(\alpha\) (ATE, ATT, TSM, AdditiveShift, Monte-Carlo’d stochastic interventions), the per-row gradient and Hessian have closed forms. xgboost’s custom objective consumes them directly.
Standard regularization. max_depth, learning_rate, early_stopping_rounds, and reg_lambda work as they do in regression. See diagnostics and tuning for \(\hat\alpha\)-specific tuning advice.
crumbs: What's a Riesz representer, and why boost it?

title: What’s a Riesz representer, and why boost it?
text:
What rieszboost provides
A scikit-learn estimator (and an R6 mirror) that:

Boosts \(\hat\alpha\) for ATE, ATT, TSM, AdditiveShift, LocalShift, StochasticIntervention, or your own estimand.
Composes with cross_val_predict, GridSearchCV, Pipeline.
Surfaces near-positivity and outlier-extrapolation warnings via .diagnose(X).

Next: install and the quickstart.
crumbs: What's a Riesz representer, and why boost it?

title: R interface
text:
The R wrapper is an R6 class that calls the Python RieszBooster through reticulate. Predictions are bitwise-identical to the Python results on the same input.
The R API is covered throughout the guide via the bilingual tabsets: see install for setup, quickstart for the standard fit pattern, and estimands for the full estimand inventory. This page is the focused R reference.

title: R interface
text:
Setup
After installing rieszboost into a Python venv (see install):
# Option A: env var, set before R starts
Sys.setenv(RETICULATE_PYTHON = "/path/to/rieszboost/.venv/bin/python")

# Option B: explicit, after R has started
use_python_rieszboost("/path/to/rieszboost/.venv/bin/python")
use_python_rieszboost() accepts a path to a Python interpreter or a virtualenv directory and forwards to the appropriate reticulate function. It also imports the Python rieszboost module so subsequent calls have no per-call import overhead.

title: R interface
text:
API surface

  $fit(df, eval_set=)
.fit(X, eval_set=)

.score(X)

  $save(path)
.save(path)


load_riesz_booster(path)
RieszBooster.load(path)


  $py
the underlying Python object



R6’s $method() syntax maps to Python’s .method(). Integer-typed kwargs (n_estimators, max_depth, random_state, early_stopping_rounds) take an L suffix in R: n_estimators = 200L. Other kwargs are auto-converted by reticulate.

title: R interface
text:
Parity
The same fit produces the same predictions in both languages. The R test suite verifies this with tolerance = 1e-12.

PythonR



import numpy as np, pandas as pd
from rieszboost import RieszBooster, ATE

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
n = 500
x = rng.uniform(0, 1, n)
a = rng.binomial(1, 1 / (1 + np.exp(-(2 * x - 1))))
df_py = pd.DataFrame({"a": a.astype(float), "x": x})

booster_py = RieszBooster(estimand=ATE(), n_estimators=50, learning_rate=0.05,
                          max_depth=3, random_state=42).fit(df_py)
alpha_py = booster_py.predict(df_py)
alpha_py[:5]

array([ 1.4076698,  1.4764098,  1.6470399, -1.1931754,  1.1374395],
      dtype=float32)





set.seed(0)
n  <- 500
x  <- runif(n)
a  <- rbinom(n, 1, 1 / (1 + exp(-(2 * x - 1))))
df_r <- data.frame(a = as.numeric(a), x = x)

booster_r <- RieszBooster$new(
  estimand = ATE(), n_estimators = 50L, learning_rate = 0.05,
  max_depth = 3L, random_state = 42L
)
booster_r$fit(df_r)
alpha_r <- booster_r$predict(df_r)
head(alpha_r, 5)

[1]  1.212691 -1.263393  1.486148 -1.365574  1.212691





The two columns above use different RNGs (default_rng(0) versus set.seed(0)), so the df and the predictions differ between tabs. To verify parity end-to-end, fit on the same data in both languages and compare predictions row-by-row.

title: R interface
text:
Custom Python objects from R
The LinearForm tracer is Python-only. To use a custom estimand or LossSpec subclass from R, define it in Python via reticulate:
reticulate::py_run_string("
def m_my_thing(z, alpha):
    return 2.0 * (alpha(a=2, x=z['x']) - alpha(a=0, x=z['x']))

from rieszboost import Estimand
my_estimand = Estimand(feature_keys=('a', 'x'), m=m_my_thing, name='MyThing')
")

est <- reticulate::py$my_estimand
booster <- RieszBooster$new(estimand = est, n_estimators = 100L, max_depth = 3L)
booster$fit(df_r)
booster$predict(df_r)[1:5]
The same pattern works for custom LossSpec subclasses: subclass in Python and pass the instance via loss=.

title: R interface
text:
Reaching the Python object directly
booster$py is the underlying Python RieszBooster. Use it for attributes the R6 wrapper doesn’t expose:
booster <- RieszBooster$new(estimand = ATE(), n_estimators = 50L)
booster$fit(df_r)

reticulate::py_to_r(booster$py$best_iteration_)
reticulate::py_to_r(booster$py$best_score_)
reticulate::py_to_r(booster$py$base_score_)

title: Save and load
text:
A fitted RieszBooster can be persisted two ways:
Both paths round-trip every built-in estimand and loss automatically.

title: Save and load
text:
sklearn idiom: joblib.dump

PythonR



import joblib, tempfile, pathlib
import numpy as np, pandas as pd
from rieszboost import RieszBooster, ATE

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
n = 800
x = rng.uniform(0, 1, n)
pi = 1 / (1 + np.exp(-(-0.02 * x - x**2 + 4 * np.log(x + 0.3) + 1.5)))
a = rng.binomial(1, pi)
df = pd.DataFrame({"a": a.astype(float), "x": x})

booster = RieszBooster(
    estimand=ATE(),
    n_estimators=200, learning_rate=0.05, max_depth=4,
).fit(df)
preds_before = booster.predict(df)

with tempfile.TemporaryDirectory() as td:
    p = pathlib.Path(td) / "booster.pkl"
    joblib.dump(booster, p)
    loaded = joblib.load(p)
    print("predictions match :", np.array_equal(preds_before, loaded.predict(df)))

    # Loaded booster still composes with sklearn:
    from sklearn.base import clone
    cloned = clone(loaded)
    print("clone OK          :", cloned.estimand.name)

['/tmp/tmp2x7kh3i2/booster.pkl']
predictions match : True
clone OK          : ATE




For R-side workflows, use the directory format below. To pickle from R via reticulate:
joblib <- reticulate::import("joblib")
joblib$dump(booster$py, "booster.pkl")
loaded_py <- joblib$load("booster.pkl")
# Wrap loaded_py back into an R6 RieszBooster if needed.

title: Save and load
text:
Portable directory format

PythonR



import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import tempfile, pathlib
from rieszboost import RieszBooster, ATE

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
n = 800
x = rng.uniform(0, 1, n)
pi = 1 / (1 + np.exp(-(-0.02*x - x**2 + 4*np.log(x + 0.3) + 1.5)))
a = rng.binomial(1, pi)
df = pd.DataFrame({"a": a.astype(float), "x": x})

booster = RieszBooster(
    estimand=ATE(),
    n_estimators=200, learning_rate=0.05, max_depth=4,
    early_stopping_rounds=20, validation_fraction=0.2,
).fit(df)
preds_before = booster.predict(df)

with tempfile.TemporaryDirectory() as td:
    save_path = pathlib.Path(td) / "my_alpha"
    booster.save(save_path)
    print("Saved files:", sorted(p.name for p in save_path.iterdir()))

    loaded = RieszBooster.load(save_path)
    preds_after = loaded.predict(df)
    print(f"predictions match exactly: {np.array_equal(preds_before, preds_after)}")
    print(f"best_iteration preserved : {loaded.best_iteration_}")

Saved files: ['booster.ubj', 'metadata.json']
predictions match exactly: True
best_iteration preserved : 199





set.seed(0)
n  <- 800
x  <- runif(n)
pi <- 1 / (1 + exp(-(-0.02 * x - x^2 + 4 * log(x + 0.3) + 1.5)))
a  <- rbinom(n, 1, pi)
df <- data.frame(a = as.numeric(a), x = x)

booster <- RieszBooster$new(
  estimand = ATE("a", "x"),
  n_estimators = 200L, learning_rate = 0.05, max_depth = 4L,
  early_stopping_rounds = 20L, validation_fraction = 0.2
)
booster$fit(df)
preds_before <- booster$predict(df)

td <- tempfile()
booster$save(td)
cat("Saved files:", paste(list.files(td), collapse = ", "), "\n")

Saved files: booster.ubj, metadata.json 

loaded <- load_riesz_booster(td)
preds_after <- loaded$predict(df)
cat(sprintf("predictions match exactly: %s\n",
            isTRUE(all.equal(preds_before, preds_after))))

predictions match exactly: TRUE

unlink(td, recursive = TRUE)

title: Save and load
text:
Cross-language
Save in one language, load in the other. The xgboost model is xgboost’s native UBJSON, and the metadata sidecar is plain JSON.
# In R: save the booster
booster$save("alpha_ate")
# In Python: load the same directory and continue working
loaded = RieszBooster.load("alpha_ate")
loaded.predict(new_df)
The R parity test exercises both directions with tolerance = 1e-12. The Python suite asserts that predictions are bitwise-identical pre- and post-save.

title: Save and load
text:
Custom estimands
Custom (non-built-in) Estimands can’t be reconstructed from JSON — the user’s m() callable doesn’t survive a save/load cycle. The booster file itself is fine; you just have to re-pass the estimand on load:

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import tempfile, pathlib
from rieszboost import RieszBooster, Estimand

# Set up data and a custom estimand (not a built-in)
rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
n = 200
df = pd.DataFrame({"a": rng.binomial(1, 0.5, n).astype(float),
                   "x": rng.uniform(0, 1, n)})

def m_custom(z, alpha):
    return alpha(a=1, x=z["x"]) - alpha(a=0, x=z["x"])

custom = Estimand(feature_keys=("a", "x"), m=m_custom, name="my_custom")
booster = RieszBooster(estimand=custom, n_estimators=20).fit(df)

with tempfile.TemporaryDirectory() as td:
    p = pathlib.Path(td) / "custom"
    booster.save(p)

    # Loading without the original estimand raises:
    try:
        RieszBooster.load(p)
    except ValueError as e:
        print("expected error:", str(e)[:80], "...")

    # Pass the original estimand to reconstruct:
    loaded = RieszBooster.load(p, estimand=custom)
    print("loaded with custom estimand OK")

expected error: Saved booster at /tmp/tmp16m6v1pk/custom has a custom (non-built-in) estimand. P ...
loaded with custom estimand OK

title: Save and load
text:
What’s in the file

booster.ubj (XGBoost backend) or predictor.joblib (sklearn-compatible backend).
metadata.json containing:

predictor_kind: "xgboost" or "sklearn".
loss: {"type": "SquaredLoss" | "KLLoss", "args": {...}} — re-instantiated with loss_from_spec(...) on load.
estimand_factory_spec: {"factory": "ATE" | ...} for built-ins, null for custom.
feature_keys / extra_keys, base_score, best_iteration, best_score.
hyperparameters — informational; lets get_params(deep=False) on the loaded booster mirror what you fit with.


The JSON is plain text and diffable; if the file format ever changes, rieszboost_format_version lets us migrate.
